top_items.title: Towards Retrieving Interaction Spaces for Agentic Search

definition: A classic lexical ranking function that scores a document from its overlapping query terms, weighting each by rarity, saturating the contribution of repeated occurrences, and correcting for document length. Those two corrections over tf-idf, saturation and length-normalization, are what make it the hard-to-beat baseline every new retrieval method is measured against, and its blind spot is vocabulary mismatch.

body.text: BM25 is the workhorse ranking function of keyword search, the default scorer in widely used engines and the benchmark every new retrieval method is measured against. It is a bag-of-words method: it judges a document's relevance to a query purely from which query terms appear and how often, with no understanding of meaning. Despite that simplicity it is remarkably effective, and it remains the canonical example of sparse retrieval, where documents and queries are represented by the explicit words they contain rather than by learned dense vectors.

BM25 refines the older tf-idf idea with two corrections that make it robust, and those corrections are the substance. The first is term-frequency saturation: a word that appears many times in a document should boost its score, but with diminishing returns, so the tenth occurrence adds far less than the second, BM25 passing the raw count through a saturating function tuned by a parameter so repetition cannot run away with the score. The second is length normalization: a long document naturally contains more words and would otherwise be unfairly favored, so BM25 discounts term frequencies by how much longer the document is than the corpus average, with a second parameter controlling how strongly. As in tf-idf, each term is also weighted by an inverse-document-frequency factor, so a rare, discriminating word counts for much more than a common one, and the document's score is the sum of these per-term contributions over the query terms.

These properties are why BM25 is hard to beat as a baseline and why it stays useful in the era of embeddings. It is exact on rare tokens, identifiers, product codes, and names, the very cases where dense retrieval can blur things together, and it needs no training, no GPU, and no embedding model, only an inverted index. It does, however, fail on vocabulary mismatch: a query and a relevant document that share no words score zero, no matter how related their meaning, because BM25 cannot see synonyms or paraphrase.

That blind spot is exactly what dense semantic search covers, and the two are complementary rather than competing, which is why modern retrieval so often runs them together. Hybrid search fuses a BM25 lexical score with a dense-vector similarity score, combining BM25's precision on exact terms with embeddings' grasp of meaning, frequently followed by a reranking pass over the merged candidates.

image.alt: A magnifier reading a query points to a stack of documents with one passage highlighted; labeled weight plus length and titled BM25.
image.caption: BM25 is the workhorse keyword ranking function: it scores a document for a query by how often the query's terms appear, dampened so repeated words give diminishing returns, and adjusted for length so long documents do not win by sheer size. Decades on, it remains a stubbornly strong baseline that dense retrievers struggle to beat outright.
